In [31]:
# Use seaborn to create a jointplot to compare the Time on Website and Yearly Amount Spent columns.

plt.figure(figsize=(10,10))
sns.jointplot(x = df['Time_on_Website'], y = df['Yearly_Amount_Spent'])
plt.show()

<Figure size 500x2000 with 0 Axes>
In [53]:
# Is there a correlation?
# Ans: There is weak correlation.
corr = df['Time_on_Website'].corr(df['Yearly_Amount_Spent'])
corr
Out[53]:
np.float64(-0.00264084471796325)

In [50]:
# Do the same as above but now with Time on App and Yearly Amount Spent

sns.jointplot(x = df['Time_on_App'], y = df['Yearly_Amount_Spent'])
plt.show()
In [54]:
# Is this correlation stronger than 1st One?
# Ans: Yes
corr = df['Time_on_App'].corr(df['Yearly_Amount_Spent'])
corr
Out[54]:
np.float64(0.49932777005983486)
In [59]:
# Explore types of relationships across the entire data set using pairplot . Based off this plot what looks to be the most correlated feature with Yearly Amount Spent?
# Ans : Length of Membership looks to be the most correlated feature with Yearly Amount Spent

sns.pairplot(df)
plt.show()
In [58]:
# Create linear model plot of Length of Membership and Yearly Amount Spent. Does the data fits well in linear plot?
# Ans : Yes the data fits quite well in linear plot.
sns.lmplot(x = 'Length_of_Membership', y = 'Yearly_Amount_Spent', data = df)
plt.show()

In [77]:
# Train and Test the data and answer multiple questions - What is the use of random_state=85
# Ans : random_state=85 ensures that train_test splits remain same everytime we run the code which makes the results reproducible.
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.25, random_state=85)
print(x.shape)
print(y.shape)
print(x_train.shape)
print(x_test.shape)
print(y_train.shape)
print(y_test.shape)

(500, 1)
(500, 1)
(375, 1)
(125, 1)
(375, 1)
(125, 1)
In [79]:
# Predict the data and do a scatter plot. Check if actual and predicted data match?

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
lr = LinearRegression()
lr.fit(x_train, y_train)

result = pd.concat([pd.DataFrame(y_test, columns=['Actual Values']), 
                    pd.DataFrame(predictions, columns=['Predicted Values']),
                    pd.DataFrame(diff, columns=['Difference'])], axis=1)
result

plt.scatter(x_test, y_test, c='orange', s = 20, label='Actual Value')
plt.scatter(x_test, predictions, c='k', s=10, label='Predicted Value')

plt.xlabel('Length of Membership')
